coredata.dc:title: GSH-AuAg NCs based fluorescent probe for multiple indicators in tap water: Free chlorine and copper ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dongning
Wang, Hao
Li, Chunye
Song, Xiaoting
Liu, Xiangping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, fluorescent AuAg bimetallic nanoclusters (AuAg NCs) were synthesized using a simple green hydrothermal method taking GSH as template. The NCs exhibits high quantum yield and stable FL performance based on aggregation induced emission (AIE). The product was mainly characterized by microscopic measurements, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, and the quenching mechanism of free chlorine and Cu2+ was studied in detail. By optimizing detection conditions and incorporating masking agents, this fluorescent probe demonstrates specific detection capabilities for free chlorine and Cu2+ specifically with the limits of detection of 0.1 μM and 0.5 nM, respectively. The proposed detection method was validated included linearity, accuracy, precision, selectivity and stability, demonstrating satisfactory recovery rates for the detection of free chlorine and Cu2+ in tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
AuAg NCs
Free chlorine detection
Cu2+ detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The feasibility study of multi-dimensional detection
The multimodal analysis of free chlorine and metal ion

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youth Foundation of Nanjing Municipal Center for Disease Control and Prevention

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authros acknowledge the support from the Youth Foundation of Nanjing Municipal Center for Disease Control and Prevention (NPY2309).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The high-resolution TEM images of AuAg NCs (A and B). The XPS (C) survey spectra and high-resolution spectra of (D) Au 4f, (E) Ag 3d of GSH-AuAg NCs. Inset in (A) shows the distribution of particle-size.

(A) Excitation spectrum of GSH-AuAg NCs (black, λem = 572 nm), GSH-Au NCs (red, λem = 572 nm) and the emission spectrum of GSH-AuAg NCs (blue, λex = 375 nm), GSH-Au NCs (cyan, λex = 375 nm). (B) UV-vis absorption spectrum of H2O, GSH, GSH-AuAg NCs, and GSH-AuAg NCs in the existence of free chlorine or/and Cu2+. (C) The fluorescence decay curves of GSH-AuAg NCs (black), in the presence of free chlorine (red) or Cu2+ (blue). The time-dependent (D), pH-dependent (E) and temperature-dependent (F) fluorescence intensity change (at 572 nm) of GSH-AuAg NCs (black), in the presence of 500 μM free chlorine (red) or 1 μM Cu2+ (blue). The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
(A and B) The fluorescence response of GSH-AuAg NCs upon addition of AA, GSH, Cu2+, free chlorine and EDTA-2Na.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs in PBS (pH = 4) with different amount of free chlorine (λex = 375 nm). Insert is the corresponding plot of the fluorescence intensity versus free chlorine concentration. (B) The linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and free chlorine concentration in the range of 3 to 100 μM. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs in PBS (pH = 7) with different amount of Cu2+ (λex = 375 nm). (D) The linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 10 to 1000 nM. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (E) Selectivity of fluorescence probe for free chlorine and Cu2+ over other ions. The concentration of free chlorine and Cu2+ were 100 and 1 μM respectively, the concentration of other ions were all 1 mM. (F) Plot of fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs within 90 days. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.
The principle of the detection mechanism.
Comparison of recent FL methods for the determination of free chlorine.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: GQD-PAN: Graphene quantum dots in polyacrylonitrile nanofibrous membranes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of recent optical methods for the determination of Cu2+.
Detection of free chlorine content in real samples.
Detection of Cu2+ content in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: Not detected: The detection result is less than the detection limit.

head.ce:title: GSH-AuAg NCs based fluorescent probe for multiple indicators in tap water: Free chlorine and copper ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Municipal Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Nanjing, 210003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing Municipal Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing Municipal Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Nanjing, 210003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, fluorescent AuAg bimetallic nanoclusters (AuAg NCs) were synthesized using a simple green hydrothermal method taking GSH as template. The NCs exhibits high quantum yield and stable FL performance based on aggregation induced emission (AIE). The product was mainly characterized by microscopic measurements, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, and the quenching mechanism of free chlorine and Cu2+ was studied in detail. By optimizing detection conditions and incorporating masking agents, this fluorescent probe demonstrates specific detection capabilities for free chlorine and Cu2+ specifically with the limits of detection of 0.1 μM and 0.5 nM, respectively. The proposed detection method was validated included linearity, accuracy, precision, selectivity and stability, demonstrating satisfactory recovery rates for the detection of free chlorine and Cu2+ in tap water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between water quality and human health is of paramount importance. Numerous detection indicators for drinking water are outlined in the Guidelines for drinking-water quality published by the World Health Organization (WHO) [1]. The safety of drinking water is being increasingly threatened by the presence of metal ions, disinfection byproducts (DBPs), per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs), antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) and so on [2,3]. The monitoring of sanitary indicators of drinking water in daily life is crucial for safeguarding public health.

Free chlorine, which consists of dissolved chlorine (Cl2), hypochlorous acid (HClO), and hypochlorite (ClO–), is the byproduct of chlorination disinfection process. Elevated levels of free chlorine have been linked to various health issues such as atherogenesis, irreversible damage to red blood cells, and cardiovascular disease [4]. The WHO specifies that the maximum allowable free chlorine concentration in drinking water is 0.5 mg/L (14 μM), while the free chlorine level in pool water should not exceed 5.0 mg/L [5,6]. Colorimetric methods using N-N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) as developer is widely-used conventional method, but existing defects of toxic reagents and tedious operations [7].

Several novel fluorescence (FL) reagents have been developed for the detection of free chlorine due to its powerful oxidizing property. The FL probes utilized for free chlorine analysis can be categorized into four main groups: 1) FL carbon quantum dots (CDs), particularly heteroatom (N, S)-doped CDs, where free chlorine can disrupt the passivation layer of CDs and alter surface defect states, leading to FL quenching [8]; 2) Metal nanoclusters (NCs) such as Au NCs [9], Cu NCs [10] and AuAg NCs [11]; 3) Diamino group-functionalized water-soluble metal-organic framework, the detection mechanism was based on the energy transfer through hydrogen bonding interactions between NH4 and ClO– [4]; 4) Organic dye, a series of small-molecule organic FL probes were applied based on the specific oxidation/chlorination reactions between free chlorine and moieties such as oxime and sulfur-containing motifs p-aminophenol [12].

Moreover, heavy metal contamination is prevalent in numerous water systems and has detrimental impacts on human health [13]. Cu2+ are sourced from a variety of origins and exhibit high mobility, exceeding the permissible levels of Cu2+ in human body can result in harm to nervous, digestive, skin and hematopoietic systems. The WHO has set a limit of 1.3 μg/L for Cu2+ level in tap water [6]. Advanced analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) are commonly employed for monitoring the concentration of Cu2+, but existing problems like high cost, complex operation and inconvenient movement apparently [14]. The paramagnetic characteristics of Cu2+ contribute to its intrinsic quenching effects on FL [15]. Consequently, cost-effective and rapid methods for Cu2+ detection, such as electrochemical sensors and optical sensors, have garnered significant interest within the scientific community [16,17].

Protein-directed Au NCs is formed by the Au-S bond between -SH and Au, due to aggregation induced emission (AIE), which possesses high quantum yield (QY), favorable biocompatibility and exceptional stability. Additionally, Ag shares a similar crystal structure and lattice constant with Au, slight dropping of Ag could improve the FL performance of Au NCs dramatically, which is so-called “Ag effect” [18]. Consequently, FL AuAg NCs was an out-standing candidate materials for optical sensors, and widely used in cell imaging, physicochemical analysis, and biological studies [19,20]. For instance, Zhang and coworkers utilized bovine serum albumin (BSA) as template to prepare BSA-AuAg NCs, which exhibited a 6.5-fold increase in FL intensity compared to BSA-Au NCs, the BSA-AuAg NCs were able to effectively detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ in blood samples [21]. Additionally, Zhang et al. used BSA-AuAg NCs to detect hypochlorite in tap water [11]. Our research group also successfully developed FL AuAg NCs using glutathione (GSH, Fig. S1, Supporting Information, SI) as temple for the detection of hemoglobin in blood samples [22].

Inspired by research results above, herein, orange-emitting light AuAg NCs was synthesized taking GSH as both capping and reducing agent by a facile hydrothermal method. Upon utilizing these GSH-AuAg NCs to detect 21 different types of common ions in water, it was observed that free chlorine and Cu2+, two prevalent pollutants in tap water, exhibiting significant quenching effect. Thanks to inherent characteristic and different quenching mechanism of two substances, reducing agent like ascorbic acid (AA) and GSH can eliminate the FL quenching caused by free chlorine. While ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), a chelating agent, can reverse fluorescence quenching caused by metal ions. By manipulating the pH of the reaction environment and incorporating appropriate masking agents, the probe was successfully used to detecte free chlorine and Cu2+ in tap water respectively.
These contents are reported in SI.

The product was prepared based on our previous reported method with slight modify [22]. Specifically, 1.5 mL of GSH was added to 7.5 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the dropwise addition of 0.9 mL of AuClH4 and 0.1 mL of AgNO3 under vigorous stirring at 25 ℃. The color of the mixture changed from colorless to white. The concentrations of GSH, AuClH4 and AgNO3 were all 20 mM, with a volume ratio of 1.5: 0.9: 0.1. After 5 min, the mixture was heated to 85 ℃ and stirred for 4.5 h resulting in a gradual color change to light yellow. The final product was then stored in the dark at 4 ℃. The GSH-Au NCs was prepared by adding 1.0 mL of HAuCl4 without AgNO3, while the other experimental conditions were kept unchanged.

In the experimental procedure, 25 μL of GSH-AuAg NCs solution and 250 μL of free chlorine/Cu2+ standard solution with varying concentrations were introduced into 2.5 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Following a 10-minute incubation period at room temperature, the FL emission spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs was recorded under an excitation wavelength of 375 nm, with excitation and emission band-slits set at 10 nm. FL intensity change of GSH-AuAg NCs at around 570 nm was correlated with the concentration of targets, which is the basis for constructing calibration curve.

The fresh tap water samples were obtained from our laboratory. For free chlorine detection, EDTA-2Na was added in the sample in prior; For Cu2+ detection, AA was added in the sample in prior. Then 250 μL of treated sample was added to PBS at different pH levels, following the same detection procedure as with standard solutions. The reliability of this method was confirmed through conventional DPD colorimetric analysis and AAS.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image illustrates the uniform diminutive size and dispersed distribution of the product, with an average size of 2.05 nm (Fig. 1 A, Fig. 1A). The NCs exhibits distinct lattice stripes, with a measured lattice spacing value of 0.235 nm, matched well with the (111) facet of Au (d = 0.2355) and Ag (d = 0.2359), as depicted in Fig. 1B [23].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to evaluate the surface valence state of the product. The survey spectra confirms the presence of C, N, O, S, Au and Ag (Fig. 1C), indicating the GSH serves as a capping agent covering the surface of AuAg NCs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Au 4f (Fig. 1D) reveals a stronger couple of peaks appeared at 84.73 and 88.48 eV, which can be ascribed to Au(Ⅰ), accompanied by another weaker couple at 83.90 and 87.93 eV, originated from Au(0) species [24]. Besides, two peaks at 367.64 and 373.81 eV are observed in the high-resolution Ag3d XPS spectra, which are well assigned to metallic Ag(Ⅰ) species (Fig. 1E) [25]. The XPS results indicate that the surface of NCs is mostly univalent metal.

To elucidate the optical properties of GSH-AuAg NCs, UV-vis absorption and FL spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the excitation wavelength of both GSH-Au NCs and GSH-AuAg NCs are 375 nm, the sharp peak appeared at 285 nm is mostly octave peak due to FL doubling effect. When stimulated at a wavelength of 375 nm, GSH-AuAg NCs exhibit a noticeable emission peak at 572 nm, in accordance with prior studies [18]. Additionally, the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs was determined to be 8.06 times higher than that of GSH-Au NCs, the absolute QY of GSH-AuAg NCs is 13.05 % measured by steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer, which is 2.15 times of GSH-Au NCs (6.07 %), these observation suggests that the presence of Ag has a notable FL enhancement effect on GSH-Au NCs, commonly referred as the “Ag effect”.

The UV-vis absorption spectra of GSH and GSH-AuAg NCs shows no absorption peak (Fig. 2B), a stronger background scattering for GSH-AuAg NCs at wavelengths < 450 nm was due to the larger size of the aggregates [24]. After adding free chlorine and Cu2+, distinct UV peaks were observed at 290 and 297 nm respectively, these wavelengths remained consistent across varying concentrations (Fig. S2), with the peak shifting to 305 nm in a mixed solution containing both free chlorine and Cu2+. Prior research indicates that GSH-AuAg NCs exhibit a absorption peak near 300 nm, with the close aggregation impacting the absorption properties of AuAg NCs. The presence of free chlorine and Cu2+ is shown to disrupt the hydration shell of Au(I)Ag(I)-thiolate complexes, alter the aggregation state of GSH-AuAg NCs, and restore the UV-vis absorption [18,21].

To further explore the FL property of the product, the fluorescence lifetime was invested. Fig. 2C displayed the time-resolved FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs in the absence and presence of free chlorine and Cu2+, the excitation wavelength was 375 nm while the emission wavelength was 572 nm. The average lifetime of GSH-AuAg NCs (τDA) was calculated to be 3.63 ns, the fast relaxation rate with nanoseconds is caused by singlet transitions inside core of AuAg NCs [26]. In addition, the FL lifetime was extended to 4.26 and 4.58 ns after adding free chlorine and Cu2+ respectively, which suggested the dynamic quenching occurred between GSH-AuAg NCs and free chlorine/Cu2+ [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility study of multi-dimensional detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investing the optimum detection condition, the influence of reaction time, pH and temperature on the detection capability of GSH-AuAg NCs were discussed. At pH of 7, the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs has almost no change during 30 min, and the RSD of 10 measurements is 2.37 % (Fig. 2D). The FL intensity quenched about 50 % as soon as adding 500 μM of free chlorine, the signal intensity decreased constantly over time and barely changed after 5 min. A comparable trend was noted in the detection of Cu2+, where the introduction of 1 μM of Cu2+ resulted in a 77 % quenching of FL intensity, which stabilized after 5 min. Consequently, the optimal acquisition time point for signal measurement was determined to be 10 min post-additive introduction.

Fig. 2E illustrates the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs with different additive at different pH levels. The intensity almost has no change in the pH range of 3–10. Upon the addition of 500 μM NaClO, quenching efficiency decreased from 84 % at pH 3 to 40 % at pH 10. NaClO would exist as HClO rather than ClO− at pH values below 7.5 in PBS, the oxidation ability of HClO is superior to ClO−, resulting in more pronounced quenching, so the detection pH of free chlorine is chosen as 4 [8]. The pH also has important effect on the quenching effect of Cu2+, 1 μM Cu2+ almost has no influence on the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs in strong acidic environment, but the quenching efficiency gets to maximum of 77 % at pH 7. Considering the pH of general tap water is between 6.5 and 8, the detection pH of Cu2+ is chosen as 7.

The temperature exerts a significant influence on the fluorescence performance of GSH-AuAg NCs. As demonstrated in Fig. 2F, following incubation at various temperatures (4, 14, 25, 37, 50 ℃) for 10 min, the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs decreased with rising temperature. In contrast, the quenching efficiency of free chlorine (500 μM, 83 %) and Cu2+ (1 μM, 75 %) remained relatively consistent across different temperature conditions.

To investigate the quenching mechanism of NaClO and Cu2+, control experiments were conducted. Fig. 3 A shows the FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs with different additive, the peak near 420 nm is the Raman peak of water (Fig. S3) [11]. Ascorbic acid (AA, 10 mM) alone has no effect on the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs in PBS (pH = 4), but 500 μM of free chlorine leads to 83 % of FL quenching, and the FL intensity almost has no change after adding 10 mM of AA, indicating the quenching process of free chlorine is irreversible. Reductant GSH also shows same result, confirming the quenching effect of free chlorine is based on the redox reactions furtherly, which could be eliminated by adding reductant in advance. Moreover, addition of EDTA-2Na has no influence on the quenching effect of free chlorine.

For Cu2+(Fig. 3B), single EDTA-2Na has no effect on the FL performance of GSH-AuAg NCs, similar to mixed solution containing Cu2+ and EDTA-2Na. 1 μM of Cu2+ would lead to about 75 % FL quenching, after adding 10 mM of EDTA-2Na, metal chelate precipitates would be formed and “turn on” the FL, restore to 93 % of the origin, the subsequent addition of AA has no influence on the FL intensity.

It is well-established that aggregation induced emission (AIE) encompasses solvent induced aggregation, assembly induced enhancement and ion induced aggregation [28]. The aggregation, facilitated by oligomeric Au(I)-thiolate complexes and the aurophilic interaction of Au(I)/Ag(I) on the surface of NCs, can result in highly intense luminescence, the intensity and color of the luminescence is dependent on the degree of aggregation [24].Varying the polarity of the solvent has been proved to be an effective method to modulate the degree of aggregation in NCs, as shown in Fig. S4, the FL of GSH-AuAg NCs complexes is significantly enhanced by adjusting the degree of molecular aggregates through the addition of ethanol, which confirmed the FL of GSH-AuAg NCs was based on AIE.

Depend on the discussion above, the detection principle was shown in Scheme. 1 . NaClO would turn to HClO in strong acid environment, which could contribute to the oxidation of reduced GSH, leading to excellent quenching efficiency on GSH-AuAg NCs (Ⅰ) [29]. So, the pre-addition of reductant AA could prevent the FL quenching (Ⅱ). In case of Cu2+, the formation of GSH-Cu2+ complex based on the bonding between carboxyl and Cu2+, destroying the aggregation of NCs. EDTA-2Na exhibits an higher affinity to Cu2+ than GSH, which could chelate with Cu2+ and restore the fluorescence no matter add order (Ⅲ and Ⅳ) [21]. In conclusion, the quenching mechanism of free chlorine and Cu2+ on GSH-AuAg NCs is totally different, reductant like AA could eliminate the quenching effect of free chlorine but have no influence on Cu2+ detection, chelating agent like EDTA-2Na could eliminate the quenching effect of Cu2+ but has no influence on free chlorine detection. Based on this, GSH-AuAg NCs could be used for separate detection of free chlorine and Cu2+ in complex environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The multimodal analysis of free chlorine and metal ion

ce:section.ce:para: The FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs in PBS (pH = 4) with different concentration of free chlorine was record (Fig. 4 A), the FL quenching degree (F0-F)/F0 increased with the addition of free chlorine, reaching 100 % at 800 μM. The evolution of FL intensity is very suitable for the determination of free chlorine within a range of 3 to 100 μM (Fig. 4B), the linear equation is y = 0.3777x + 13.7857 (R2 = 0.9926, S/N = 3) with a LOD of 0.1 μM. Table 1 summarizes the recent FL methods for free chlorine assay, this sensing system shows comparable detection range and LOD.

Fig. 4C shows the FL spectra of GSH-AuAg NCs with different concentration of Cu2+ in PBS at pH 7. The FL intensity decreases gradually with increasing of Cu2+, the calibration curve for Cu2+ was established according to the FL quenching efficiency. Regression equation is calculated to be y = 0.0785x + 0.5428 (R2 = 0.9987) in a wide range of 10–1000 nM, and the LOD at 3σ is 0.5 nM (Fig. 4D). This method is comparable to many Cu2+ optical assays ever reported (Table 2 ).

Despite the reductant (AA) and metal-chelator (EDTA-2Na), the GSH-AuAg NCs exhibits robust anti-interference capabilities against usual inorganic and metal ions in water. Specifically, the presence of 0.1 mM NaClO results in approximately 50 % quenching, while 0.1 μM of Cu2+ leads to around 80 % quenching, as depicted in Fig. 4E. In contrast, 0.1 mM of common metal positive ions (Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Pb2+) and negative ions (Cl−, F−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, C2O4 2−), almost have no influence on the FL intensity of GSH-AuAg NCs. Notably, the FL intensity remains about 70 % of its initial value in the presence of 0.1 mM Hg2+, attributed to the metalophilic bond between Hg2+ and Au 5d10 center, and the disturb could be eliminated by adding EDTA-2Na (Fig. S5) [39]. Fortunately, the concentration of Hg2+ is relatively low in common tap water, which would not affect the testing result. The anti-interference experiment suggests that the FL probe might be used to detect multi-index (free chlorine and Cu2+) in real water samples directly.

Therefore, practical application of GSH-AuAg NCs in free chlorine and Cu2+ detection in tap water was conduct by a standard addition method, the results were compared with conventional method. The determination results along with recovery values of the spiked samples were listed in Table 3 and 4
. Stability test was also conducted to investigate the feasibility of this FL probe, as shown in Fig. 4F, the FL intensity keeps 93 % after 3 months stored at low temperature away from light. The findings suggest the proposed GSH-AuAg NCs is a promising FL probe for detection of free chlorine and metal ions in tap water with excellent accuracy, precision, and sensitivity.

This study introduces a new FL material, GSH-AuAg NCs, for the sensitive detection of two different indices (free chlorine and Cu2+) in tap water. The quenching effect of free chlorine on GSH-AuAg NCs is attributed to redox reaction, while the quenching of Cu2+ is primarily due to metal bonding. The distinct quenching mechanisms enable the sensitive and selective detection of free chlorine and Cu2+ in real water samples using a single FL probe. This research broadens the potential for widespread use of novel FL NCs in monitoring water quality parameters.

Dongning Li: Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hao Wang: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Chunye Li: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Xiaoting Song: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiangping Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authros acknowledge the support from the Youth Foundation of Nanjing Municipal Center for Disease Control and Prevention (NPY2309).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115712.
